chunk_summary: Overview of microbiome test types, their pediatric interpretation limits, and practical considerations for clinical counseling, emphasizing methodological variability and lack of validated pediatric reference ranges.
learning_objectives: Understand the differences between common microbiome testing methods, including 16S rRNA sequencing and shotgun metagenomics.
Identify key limitations in interpreting microbiome test results in pediatric populations.
Learn how to use microbiome reports to guide clinical history-taking rather than prescribing interventions.
Recognize the impact of sample handling and patient factors on microbiome test results.
key_facts: 16S rRNA sequencing reports relative abundance and diversity indices, while shotgun metagenomics provides species-level profiles and predicted genes.
Microbiome results are highly sensitive to sample timing, storage, antibiotics, diet changes, and infant age.
No universally validated pediatric reference ranges exist for most taxa, especially in children under 3 years.
Dysbiosis scores lack proven links to child-important outcomes or specific interventions.
Metagenomic functional predictions should not be treated as clinical diagnoses without independent testing.
reference_quote: A comparison between 16S rRNA sequencing and shotgun metagenomics for the gut (https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-021-82726-y).
document_key_topics: Clinical counseling based on microbiome reports
